participants.extracted: The study enrolled 105 overweight and mildly obese adults from faculty, staff, and student populations at three U.S. institutions (University of Tennessee, Purdue University, and USDA/ARS Western Human Nutrition Research Center, University of California, Davis). Eligibility required age 18-35 years, BMI 25-34.9 kg/m2, low baseline calcium intake, stable body weight, and no recent changes in exercise, smoking, diabetes treatment, anti-obesity medications, calcium supplementation, pregnancy/lactation, malabsorption, or eating disorders. Seventy-nine participants completed the trial, and 61 had the majority of analyzable data. The final sample included predominantly women, with 19 females and 4 males in the dairy group, 17 females and 2 males in the calcium group, and 20 females and 6 males in the placebo group; mean ages were approximately 24-25 years.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to 12 weeks of one of three energy-restricted dietary regimens: a control diet providing a 500 kcal/day deficit, 0–1 servings/day of dairy, about 500 mg calcium/day, plus a daily placebo supplement; an otherwise identical calcium-supplemented diet in which the placebo was replaced with 900 mg/day of calcium carbonate; or a high-dairy diet providing the same energy deficit with three daily servings of dairy products (milk, cheese, and/or yogurt), increasing total calcium intake to about 1400 mg/day, along with a placebo supplement. The dairy arm was unblinded, while the calcium-supplement and placebo/control arms were placebo-controlled and blinded.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure anthropometric and clinical outcomes over a 12-week intervention period. Primary body composition outcomes included percent change in body weight, percent change in waist circumference, percent change in lean mass, and percent change in body fat, assessed at baseline and week 12 using weekly body weight measurements, waist circumference at baseline and 12 weeks, and dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) for lean mass and body fat at baseline and week 12. Additional clinical/metabolic outcomes measured at baseline and week 12 included plasma glucose, triglycerides, LDL cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, fasting insulin, and insulin resistance estimated by HOMA-IR. The study also measured circulating total lipid classes and detailed plasma fatty acid composition (including cholesterol ester, free fatty acids, phosphatidylcholine, triacylglycerol, and related fatty acid species) at baseline and week 12. Dietary intake outcomes were assessed from 7-day diet records during the run-in period and repeated 3-day dietary records during weeks 0-11/12, including total energy, macronutrient composition, fatty acid composition, and selected micronutrients. Physical activity was also recorded at baseline and week 12 using 3-day activity records.
